user_question: What does DNA stand for?

messages.content: What does DNA stand for?

content.text:
DNA stands for **deoxyribonucleic acid**.

It's a molecule that carries genetic instructions for life, found in the cells of all living organisms. DNA contains the information needed to build and maintain an organism, and it's responsible for passing genetic traits from parents to offspring.

final_answer:
DNA stands for **deoxyribonucleic acid**.

It's a molecule that carries genetic instructions for life, found in the cells of all living organisms. DNA contains the information needed to build and maintain an organism, and it's responsible for passing genetic traits from parents to offspring.
golden_answer: DNA stands for deoxyribonucleic acid, the molecule that carries genetic information in nearly all living organisms.

user_question: How long does it take Earth to orbit the Sun?

messages.content: How long does it take Earth to orbit the Sun?

content.text:
Earth takes approximately **365.25 days** to orbit the Sun. This is why we have a standard year of 365 days, with an extra day added every 4 years (leap year) to account for that extra quarter day.

More precisely, Earth's orbital period is about **365.2422 days**, which is also referred to as a "sidereal year" (the time it takes Earth to complete one full orbit relative to the stars).

final_answer:
Earth takes approximately **365.25 days** to orbit the Sun. This is why we have a standard year of 365 days, with an extra day added every 4 years (leap year) to account for that extra quarter day.

More precisely, Earth's orbital period is about **365.2422 days**, which is also referred to as a "sidereal year" (the time it takes Earth to complete one full orbit relative to the stars).
golden_answer: Earth takes approximately 365.25 days to complete one orbit around the Sun, which is why we add a leap day every four years.

user_question: Who is considered the father of modern physics?

messages.content: Who is considered the father of modern physics?

content.text:
Albert Einstein is widely considered the father of modern physics. He revolutionized our understanding of space, time, gravity, and energy through his groundbreaking theories of relativity (both special and general relativity) and his work on the photoelectric effect. His famous equation E=mc² fundamentally changed how we understand the relationship between mass and energy, and his theories laid the foundation for much of 20th-century physics, including quantum mechanics and cosmology.

Einstein's work in the early 1900s transformed physics from classical Newtonian mechanics into the modern era of physics that we study today.

final_answer:
Albert Einstein is widely considered the father of modern physics. He revolutionized our understanding of space, time, gravity, and energy through his groundbreaking theories of relativity (both special and general relativity) and his work on the photoelectric effect. His famous equation E=mc² fundamentally changed how we understand the relationship between mass and energy, and his theories laid the foundation for much of 20th-century physics, including quantum mechanics and cosmology.

Einstein's work in the early 1900s transformed physics from classical Newtonian mechanics into the modern era of physics that we study today.
golden_answer: Albert Einstein is often called the father of modern physics, particularly for his theories of special and general relativity.

Crunchyroll licensed the season outside of Asia and streamed it along with an English dub two weeks after the original airing on its streaming service of the same name. Medialink licensed the season in Asia-Pacific.

user_question: What's the smallest planet in our solar system?

messages.content: What's the smallest planet in our solar system?

content.text:
The smallest planet in our solar system is **Mercury**. 

Mercury has a diameter of about 3,832 kilometers (2,380 miles), making it significantly smaller than all the other planets. It's also the closest planet to the Sun. Despite being the smallest, Mercury is still quite substantial—it's about 38% the diameter of Earth.

final_answer:

golden_answer: Mercury is the smallest planet in the solar system, with a diameter of about 4,880 kilometers.

{"title": "Alexander Ovechkin", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Ovechkin", "lead": "Alexander Mikhailovich Ovechkin (Russian: Алексaндр Михайлович Овечкин, romanized: Aleksandr Mikhaylovich Ovechkin, pronounced [ɐlʲɪˈksandr ɐˈvʲetɕkʲɪn]; born 17 September 1985) is a Russian professional ice hockey player who is a left winger and captain for the Washington Capitals of the National Hockey League (NHL). Nicknamed "the Great 8" (in reference to his jersey number) and "Ovi", as well as "Alexander the Great" (Russian: Александр Великий) by Russian media, Ovechkin has scored the most career goals in NHL regular season history, and is the only player in NHL history to have scored 900 regular season goals.
Ovechkin began his professional career with Dynamo Moscow of the Russian Superleague in 2001, playing there for four seasons. Ovechkin was selected by the Capitals first overall in the 2004 NHL entry draft. In the 2005–06 season, Ovechkin captured the Calder Memorial Trophy as rookie of the year and finished third overall in league scoring.
Ovechkin has won the Maurice "Rocket" Richard Trophy as the NHL's leading goal scorer a record nine times and been runner-up once. He holds the NHL record for most 40-goal seasons with thirteen, and shares with Mike Bossy and Wayne Gretzky the record for most 50-goal campaigns with nine. He is the only player to have tallied 200 or more goals in three different decades, with 245 in the 2000s, 437 in the 2010s, and 213 in the 2020s. He also holds multiple other NHL records, including the most power play goals, most goals in away games, most overtime goals, most game-winning goals, most individual goalies scored upon, and most goals with one team.
He has won the Hart Memorial Trophy for most valuable player three times (in 2008, 2009, and 2013) while also being a finalist two other times (2010 and 2015), and the award for best player as voted on by the National Hockey League Players' Association three times (2008, 2009, and 2010) while also being a finalist two other times (2013 and 2015). In 2018, the Capitals won the Stanley Cup for the first time, and Ovechkin was awarded the Conn Smythe Trophy for most valuable player in the 2018 playoffs. He has also been named to the NHL first All-Star team eight times, and the second All-Star team four times. In 2017, Ovechkin was named one of the 100 Greatest NHL Players of all time.
Internationally, Ovechkin has represented Russia in multiple tournaments. His first IIHF tournament was the 2002 World U18 Championship. The following year he made his debut at the World Junior Championship, helping Russia win the gold medal. He played two more years at the World Juniors, as well as once more at the World U18 Championships. Ovechkin's first senior tournament was the 2004 World Championship, and he also played in the World Cup that year. Ovechkin has also played for Russia at the Winter Olympics in 2006, 2010, and 2014. Overall, Ovechkin has represented Russia at thirteen World Championships and three Olympics in his career, winning the World Championship three times.

user_question: What's the chemical symbol for gold and where does it come from?

messages.content: What's the chemical symbol for gold and where does it come from?

content.text:
The chemical symbol for gold is **Au**, which comes from its Latin name "aurum."

As for where gold comes from, gold is a naturally occurring precious metal found in the Earth's crust. It forms through several natural processes:

1. **Geological formation**: Gold is primarily created through nucleosynthesis in dying massive stars and neutron star collisions. Over billions of years, this cosmic gold became incorporated into the Earth during its formation.

2. **Concentration in the crust**: In the Earth's crust, gold is found in various geological settings:
   - **Hydrothermal veins**: Gold deposits often form in quartz veins created by hot, mineral-rich fluids flowing through rock fractures
   - **Placer deposits**: Weathering and erosion concentrate gold particles in stream beds and alluvial deposits
   - **Ore deposits**: Gold is extracted from various ore bodies, often associated with other minerals

3. **Mining**: Today, gold is extracted from ore deposits through mining operations and is also recovered as a byproduct of mining other metals like copper.

Gold is also present in very small quantities in seawater and in the human body, but these sources are not economically viable for extraction. The main sources of gold come from hard rock mining (open-pit and underground mines) and placer mining operations around the world, with major producers including China, Australia, Russia, and the United States.

final_answer:
The chemical symbol for gold is **Au**, which comes from its Latin name "aurum."

As for where gold comes from, gold is a naturally occurring precious metal found in the Earth's crust. It forms through several natural processes:

1. **Geological formation**: Gold is primarily created through nucleosynthesis in dying massive stars and neutron star collisions. Over billions of years, this cosmic gold became incorporated into the Earth during its formation.

2. **Concentration in the crust**: In the Earth's crust, gold is found in various geological settings:
   - **Hydrothermal veins**: Gold deposits often form in quartz veins created by hot, mineral-rich fluids flowing through rock fractures
   - **Placer deposits**: Weathering and erosion concentrate gold particles in stream beds and alluvial deposits
   - **Ore deposits**: Gold is extracted from various ore bodies, often associated with other minerals

3. **Mining**: Today, gold is extracted from ore deposits through mining operations and is also recovered as a byproduct of mining other metals like copper.

Gold is also present in very small quantities in seawater and in the human body, but these sources are not economically viable for extraction. The main sources of gold come from hard rock mining (open-pit and underground mines) and placer mining operations around the world, with major producers including China, Australia, Russia, and the United States.
golden_answer: Gold's chemical symbol is Au, derived from its Latin name 'aurum', meaning 'shining dawn'.

user_question: Why is the ocean salty?

messages.content: Why is the ocean salty?

content.text:
The ocean is salty because of a combination of geological and chemical processes:

1. **Weathering of rocks**: When rain falls on land, it's slightly acidic (due to dissolved carbon dioxide forming carbonic acid). This weak acid slowly dissolves minerals in rocks and soil, particularly salts containing sodium, chloride, and other mineral ions.

2. **Rivers transport salt to the ocean**: These dissolved minerals are carried by rivers and streams into the ocean. This has been happening for billions of years.

3. **Evaporation leaves salt behind**: Water evaporates from the ocean surface due to the sun's heat, but salt doesn't evaporate with it. The salt is left behind in the ocean, gradually accumulating over time.

4. **Volcanic activity**: Underwater volcanic activity and hot springs (hydrothermal vents) also release minerals and salts into the ocean water.

5. **Equilibrium state**: The ocean has reached a kind of equilibrium where salt continues to be added from rivers and volcanic sources, while some salt is removed through processes like mineral precipitation and burial in sediments on the ocean floor. The concentration of salt has remained relatively stable for a long time.

The primary salt in seawater is sodium chloride (table salt), which makes up about 85% of the dissolved salts. On average, seawater contains about 35 grams of salt per liter, or about 3.5% salinity. This salinity is one of the defining characteristics of ocean water and plays an important role in marine ecosystems and global ocean circulation patterns.

final_answer:
The ocean is salty because of a combination of geological and chemical processes:

1. **Weathering of rocks**: When rain falls on land, it's slightly acidic (due to dissolved carbon dioxide forming carbonic acid). This weak acid slowly dissolves minerals in rocks and soil, particularly salts containing sodium, chloride, and other mineral ions.

2. **Rivers transport salt to the ocean**: These dissolved minerals are carried by rivers and streams into the ocean. This has been happening for billions of years.

3. **Evaporation leaves salt behind**: Water evaporates from the ocean surface due to the sun's heat, but salt doesn't evaporate with it. The salt is left behind in the ocean, gradually accumulating over time.

4. **Volcanic activity**: Underwater volcanic activity and hot springs (hydrothermal vents) also release minerals and salts into the ocean water.

5. **Equilibrium state**: The ocean has reached a kind of equilibrium where salt continues to be added from rivers and volcanic sources, while some salt is removed through processes like mineral precipitation and burial in sediments on the ocean floor. The concentration of salt has remained relatively stable for a long time.

The primary salt in seawater is sodium chloride (table salt), which makes up about 85% of the dissolved salts. On average, seawater contains about 35 grams of salt per liter, or about 3.5% salinity. This salinity is one of the defining characteristics of ocean water and plays an important role in marine ecosystems and global ocean circulation patterns.
golden_answer: Rivers carry dissolved minerals, including salts, from rocks on land into the ocean. Over billions of years these salts accumulated, since water evaporates but the salt remains.

user_question: Who painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel?

messages.content: Who painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel?

content.text: Michelangelo painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. He completed this masterpiece between 1508 and 1512. The ceiling is one of the most famous works of Renaissance art and features scenes from the Book of Genesis, including the iconic "Creation of Adam."

final_answer: Michelangelo painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. He completed this masterpiece between 1508 and 1512. The ceiling is one of the most famous works of Renaissance art and features scenes from the Book of Genesis, including the iconic "Creation of Adam."
golden_answer: Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling between 1508 and 1512, commissioned by Pope Julius II.

content.content: {"title": "List of My Hero Academia episodes", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_My_Hero_Academia_episodes", "lead": "My Hero Academia is an anime television series based on the manga series My Hero Academia by K\u014dhei Horikoshi. An anime series adaptation was announced in Weekly Sh\u014dnen Jump in October 2015 and produced by Bones. The series is directed by Kenji Nagasaki, written by Y\u014dsuke Kuroda, featuring character designs by Yoshihiko Umakoshi who also serves as the chief animation director, and music composed by Yuki Hayashi. The story follows Izuku Midoriya, a boy born without superpowers in a world where they are the norm, but who still dreams of becoming a superhero himself, and is scouted by the world's greatest hero, All Might, who shares his powers with Izuku after recognizing his value and enrolls him in a prestigious high school for superheroes in training.\nThe first season aired from April 3 to June 26, 2016, on all JNN affiliate stations, including TBS Television and Mainichi Broadcasting System. The second season aired from April 1 to September 30, 2017, on all NNS affiliate stations going forward, including Yomiuri TV and Nippon TV, with the staff and cast from the first season returning to reprise their roles. The third season aired from April 7 to September 29, 2018. The fourth season aired from October 12, 2019, to April 4, 2020. The fifth season aired from March 27 to September 25, 2021. The sixth season aired from October 1, 2022, to March 25, 2023. The seventh season aired from May 4 to October 12, 2024, with 4 \"Memories\" recap specials having aired in the preceding month of April. The eighth and final season aired from October 4 to December 13, 2025. A bonus television special that adapts chapter 431, which was bundled with the 42nd and final volume of the manga, premiered on May 2, 2026. The series featured a total of twenty-eight different theme songs across all eight seasons: fourteen opening themes and fourteen ending themes.\nThe series is licensed in North America, United Kingdom and Ireland by Crunchyroll (formerly Funimation), and is streamed on Crunchyroll, Hulu and Netflix. Universal Pictures, Sony Pictures and Manga Entertainment distributes the series in the United Kingdom and Ireland. Universal, Sony Pictures Home Entertainment and Madman Anime are simulcasting the series in Australia and New Zealand. Medialink licensed the series in Southeast Asia. They aired it simultaneously on Animax Asia. In April 2018, it was announced that the series's English dub would air in the United States on Adult Swim's Toonami programming block starting on May 5, 2018. In August 2023, Cartoon Network announced that the series would air in India starting on September 10, 2023. The first two seasons of the series's English dub once again aired in the United States on AXS TV from February 20 to October 3, 2025, as part of Web3 company Azuki's short-lived Anime.com Hour programming block.", "error": null}

user_question: What's the longest river in the world?

messages.content: What's the longest river in the world?

content.text: The longest river in the world is the **Nile River**, located in Africa. It flows through eleven countries and spans approximately 4,130 miles (6,650 kilometers) in length. The Nile has been crucial to the development of Egyptian civilization for thousands of years, providing water, transportation, and fertile soil through its annual flooding cycles.

final_answer: The longest river in the world is the **Nile River**, located in Africa. It flows through eleven countries and spans approximately 4,130 miles (6,650 kilometers) in length. The Nile has been crucial to the development of Egyptian civilization for thousands of years, providing water, transportation, and fertile soil through its annual flooding cycles.
golden_answer: The Nile is traditionally considered the longest river at about 6,650 km, though some studies argue the Amazon is slightly longer.

user_question: What's the boiling point of water in Celsius and Fahrenheit at sea level?

messages.content: What's the boiling point of water in Celsius and Fahrenheit at sea level?

content.text:
At sea level, the boiling point of water is:

- **100°C (Celsius)**
- **212°F (Fahrenheit)**

These are the standard boiling points used in most scientific and everyday applications. The boiling point can vary slightly at different altitudes due to atmospheric pressure changes—at higher elevations with lower atmospheric pressure, water boils at lower temperatures.

final_answer:
At sea level, the boiling point of water is:

- **100°C (Celsius)**
- **212°F (Fahrenheit)**

These are the standard boiling points used in most scientific and everyday applications. The boiling point can vary slightly at different altitudes due to atmospheric pressure changes—at higher elevations with lower atmospheric pressure, water boils at lower temperatures.
golden_answer: Water boils at 100°C, which is 212°F, at standard atmospheric pressure (sea level).

user_question: How does a basic vaccine work?

messages.content: How does a basic vaccine work?

content.text:
I can explain how a basic vaccine works using my existing knowledge:

## How a Basic Vaccine Works

A vaccine is a medical preparation designed to stimulate your immune system to develop immunity against a specific disease. Here's the basic process:

### 1. **Introduction of a Pathogen or Antigen**
A vaccine introduces a weakened or inactive form of a disease-causing pathogen (like a virus or bacteria), or just a piece of it (an antigen). This could be:
- A live attenuated (weakened) virus
- An inactivated (killed) virus or bacteria
- A protein fragment from the pathogen
- Genetic instructions to make a specific protein (like in mRNA vaccines)

### 2. **Immune System Recognition**
Your immune system recognizes this foreign substance as a threat and mounts a response without causing the actual disease (or causing only a mild version of it).

### 3. **Antibody Production**
Your body produces antibodies—specialized proteins that bind to and neutralize the pathogen. Different types of immune cells are also activated and trained to recognize this specific threat.

### 4. **Memory Formation**
Crucially, your immune system creates "memory cells" that remember the pathogen. These cells remain in your body for months, years, or even a lifetime.

### 5. **Protection Against Future Infection**
If you're later exposed to the actual disease, your immune system quickly recognizes it and rapidly produces antibodies and activates the appropriate immune cells. This rapid response prevents the disease from taking hold or significantly reduces its severity.

### Why Vaccines Are Effective
Vaccines essentially provide a "practice run" for your immune system, allowing it to learn how to fight a disease without you having to suffer from the actual illness.

final_answer:

### 2. **Immune System Recognition**

### 3. **Antibody Production**

### 4. **Memory Formation**

{"title": "2025 Indianapolis 500", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Indianapolis_500", "lead": "The 2025 Indianapolis 500 (branded as the 109th Running of the Indianapolis 500 presented by Gainbridge for sponsorship reasons) was an IndyCar Series race that was held on Sunday, May 25, 2025, at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana. The race was round six of the 2025 NTT IndyCar Series season, and was televised on Fox for the first time in its history. The 2025 race marked the first year of hybrid energy recovery system powertrains at Indianapolis.
The month of May activities formally began with the Sonsio Grand Prix on the combined road course. Practice officially began on Tuesday, May 13, and Time Trials were held on May 17–18. Carb Day, the traditional final day of practice, along with the Pit Stop Challenge, took place on May 23. Rookie driver Robert Shwartzman of Prema Racing qualified for the pole position, the first rookie pole winner since Teo Fabi in 1983.
Three-time series champion Álex Palou of Chip Ganassi Racing won the race, his first Indianapolis 500 victory and first oval-track victory in IndyCar. It marked Ganassi's 6th Indy 500 victory, tied for second most all-time. It was Palou's sixth start at Indy, his best previous finish was a second in 2021. Palou took the lead on lap 187, and held off 2022 winner Marcus Ericsson of Andretti Global. Second place Ericsson, his teammate Kyle Kirkwood (6th), and Callum Ilott (12th) all failed post-race technical inspection, and were moved to the back of the field when official results were posted. Consequently, David Malukas was elevated to second place and Pato O'Ward to third. Palou's win highlighted a dominating season in which he won eight races and became the first driver to win the Indianapolis 500 and the IndyCar championship in the same season since Dario Franchitti in 2010.
Josef Newgarden, the race winner in 2023–2024, failed to accomplish the first Indianapolis 500 three-peat. Newgarden and Penske teammate Will Power were moved to the back of the starting grid due to technical infractions during qualifying. All three cars of the Penske team had disappointing results, the team's worst overall performance at Indy since failing to qualify in 1995. Power finished a lap down in 16th, Newgarden dropped out with mechanical failure, and Scott McLaughlin crashed out on the pace lap (after also crashing heavily during practice). Statistically, it was the team's worst combined three-car result at Indy since 1992.
Kyle Larson made his second attempt at "Double Duty" after failing in 2024, as that year's Coca-Cola 600 was called for rain. He crashed out at Indianapolis on lap 92. Larson would lead 34 laps early on at Charlotte, but dropped out after a crash. At season's end, Larson won the 2025 Cup Series Championship – the first time in history that a driver raced in the "500" and won the NASCAR championship in the same calendar year. Polesitter Robert Shwartzman led 8 laps, but dropped out on lap 87 after skidding into the wall entering the pits. He still was voted rookie of the year. Two-time winner Takuma Sato led the most laps, but lost the lead when he overshot his pit stall. He battled back to place 9th.", "error": null}

- **Callum Ilott** (12th place) - moved back

This had a significant impact on the final results, with **David Malukas** being elevated to 2nd place and **Pato O'Ward** to 3rd place as a result.

**Pre-Race/Qualifying Penalties:**
Additionally, **Josef Newgarden** and **Will Power** (both from Penske) were moved to the back of the starting grid before the race even began due to technical infractions during qualifying.

These penalties were particularly notable because they affected the finish order and contributed to what was described as Penske's worst combined three-car performance at Indianapolis since 1992. The three-time champion Newgarden, who had won the previous two years (2023-2024), didn't finish the race due to mechanical failure.

user_question: What's the hardest natural substance on Earth?

messages.content: What's the hardest natural substance on Earth?

content.text:
The hardest natural substance on Earth is **diamond**. 

Diamond is the hardest known natural material, ranking at the top of the Mohs hardness scale with a rating of 10. This exceptional hardness comes from its crystal structure, where each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms in an extremely strong tetrahedral arrangement. This makes diamond useful not only for jewelry but also for industrial applications like cutting, grinding, and polishing tools.

While synthetic materials like aggregated diamond nanorods have been created in laboratories and may theoretically be harder, diamond is the hardest substance found in nature.

final_answer:
The hardest natural substance on Earth is **diamond**. 

Diamond is the hardest known natural material, ranking at the top of the Mohs hardness scale with a rating of 10. This exceptional hardness comes from its crystal structure, where each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms in an extremely strong tetrahedral arrangement. This makes diamond useful not only for jewelry but also for industrial applications like cutting, grinding, and polishing tools.

While synthetic materials like aggregated diamond nanorods have been created in laboratories and may theoretically be harder, diamond is the hardest substance found in nature.
golden_answer: Diamond is the hardest naturally occurring substance, scoring a 10 on the Mohs hardness scale.

user_question: Why do we have leap years?

messages.content: Why do we have leap years?

content.text:
Leap years exist because of a mismatch between our calendar system and Earth's actual orbit around the sun.

Here's why we need them:

**The Problem:**
Earth takes approximately 365.2425 days to complete one full orbit around the sun (a tropical year). Our standard calendar year is 365 days. This means we're short by about 0.2425 days (roughly 5 hours and 49 minutes) every year.

**The Accumulation:**
Over time, these fractional days add up. Without leap years, after about 4 years we'd be missing roughly 1 full day. Over centuries, the calendar would drift significantly out of sync with the seasons. Eventually, winter would start occurring in summer months, and spring planting/harvest times would become meaningless.

**The Solution:**
To correct this drift, we add an extra day (February 29) every 4 years, creating a "leap year" with 366 days instead of 365. This extra day approximately balances out the fractional part of the year.

**Fine-tuning the System:**
However, 365.25 days is slightly more than the actual 365.2425 days. So the Gregorian calendar (used internationally today) has additional rules:
- Years divisible by 100 are NOT leap years (e.g., 1900, 2100)
- EXCEPT years divisible by 400 ARE leap years (e.g., 2000, 2400)

This complex system keeps the calendar extraordinarily accurate—off by only about 1 day every 3,000+ years. It ensures that seasonal events like equinoxes and solstices occur on roughly the same dates year after year.

final_answer:
Leap years exist because of a mismatch between our calendar system and Earth's actual orbit around the sun.

Here's why we need them:

**The Problem:**
Earth takes approximately 365.2425 days to complete one full orbit around the sun (a tropical year). Our standard calendar year is 365 days. This means we're short by about 0.2425 days (roughly 5 hours and 49 minutes) every year.

**The Accumulation:**
Over time, these fractional days add up. Without leap years, after about 4 years we'd be missing roughly 1 full day. Over centuries, the calendar would drift significantly out of sync with the seasons. Eventually, winter would start occurring in summer months, and spring planting/harvest times would become meaningless.

**The Solution:**
To correct this drift, we add an extra day (February 29) every 4 years, creating a "leap year" with 366 days instead of 365. This extra day approximately balances out the fractional part of the year.

**Fine-tuning the System:**
However, 365.25 days is slightly more than the actual 365.2425 days. So the Gregorian calendar (used internationally today) has additional rules:
- Years divisible by 100 are NOT leap years (e.g., 1900, 2100)
- EXCEPT years divisible by 400 ARE leap years (e.g., 2000, 2400)

This complex system keeps the calendar extraordinarily accurate—off by only about 1 day every 3,000+ years. It ensures that seasonal events like equinoxes and solstices occur on roughly the same dates year after year.
golden_answer: Earth's orbit takes about 365.25 days, so adding an extra day every four years keeps our calendar aligned with the astronomical seasons.

user_question: How many continents are there?

messages.content: How many continents are there?

content.text:
There are **7 continents** in the most widely used geographical model:

1. Africa
2. Antarctica
3. Asia
4. Europe
5. North America
6. Oceania (Australia)
7. South America

However, it's worth noting that the number of continents can vary depending on the model used:

- **6-continent model**: Some regions combine Europe and Asia into "Eurasia," and/or North and South America into "America," resulting in 6 continents.
- **5-continent model**: Some educational systems (particularly in some parts of Europe and Latin America) use a 5-continent model by combining continents differently.

The 7-continent model is the most commonly taught in English-speaking countries and is the standard used by most modern geography textbooks.

final_answer:
There are **7 continents** in the most widely used geographical model:

1. Africa
2. Antarctica
3. Asia
4. Europe
5. North America
6. Oceania (Australia)
7. South America

However, it's worth noting that the number of continents can vary depending on the model used:

- **6-continent model**: Some regions combine Europe and Asia into "Eurasia," and/or North and South America into "America," resulting in 6 continents.
- **5-continent model**: Some educational systems (particularly in some parts of Europe and Latin America) use a 5-continent model by combining continents differently.

The 7-continent model is the most commonly taught in English-speaking countries and is the standard used by most modern geography textbooks.
golden_answer: There are seven continents: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia (or Oceania), Europe, North America, and South America.

user_question: Who invented the telephone?

messages.content: Who invented the telephone?

content.text: Alexander Graham Bell is credited with inventing the telephone. He patented the telephone in 1876 and is widely recognized as its inventor, though there were other inventors and innovators working on similar technologies around the same time. Bell's telephone was the first practical and commercially successful telephone system.

final_answer: Alexander Graham Bell is credited with inventing the telephone. He patented the telephone in 1876 and is widely recognized as its inventor, though there were other inventors and innovators working on similar technologies around the same time. Bell's telephone was the first practical and commercially successful telephone system.
golden_answer: Alexander Graham Bell is generally credited with inventing the telephone and was awarded the first U.S. patent for it in 1876.

It is the longest race on NASCAR's schedule. It is unique for having track conditions that change throughout the race due to the race having a day to night transition, (if the race occurs on schedule with no delays or postponements). The race starts around 6 p.m. when the track is bathed in sunlight for about the first third of the race. Roughly the second third happens at dusk, and about the final third of the race occurs at night under the lights.
The race is run later on the same day as the Indianapolis 500 of the IndyCar Series, with multiple drivers having performed or attempted Double Duty, competing in both races. No driver has ever won both races, either on the same day or across their career.
The NASCAR event is held on the last weekend of May and is known as one of the largest weekends in auto racing, as the NASCAR race occurs on the same Sunday after the Canadian Grand Prix (Formula One) and the Indianapolis 500 (IndyCar Series).

Double Duty is physically demanding and mentally draining; a driver must be in a race car for most of the day, racing for more than 1,000 miles with little or no rest. It is similar in concept to the established discipline of endurance racing, but without the help of a relief driver, and the driver withstanding the very different physical demands of an IndyCar, which was historically run with an open cockpit, but since 2020 has been run with a semi-enclosed cockpit with the introduction of the aeroscreen, and a stock car, in which the driver is enclosed.

Stewart's 2001 effort stands as the best combined result: sixth at Indianapolis and third at Charlotte. {"title": "Nico Hülkenberg", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nico_H%C3%BClkenberg", "lead": "Nicolas Hülkenberg (German pronunciation: [ˈniːko ˈhʏlkənbɛɐ̯k]; born 19 August 1987) is a German racing driver who competes in Formula One for Audi. In endurance racing, Hülkenberg won the 24 Hours of Le Mans in 2015 with Porsche.
Born and raised in Emmerich am Rhein, Hülkenberg began competitive kart racing at age 10, winning several national titles before graduating to junior formulae in 2005. Hülkenberg won his first championship in Formula BMW ADAC that year. He then won the 2006–07 A1 Grand Prix series, representing Germany. After winning the 2007 Masters of Formula 3 and the 2008 Formula 3 Euro Series, Hülkenberg progressed to the GP2 Series in 2009 with ART; he won the championship in his debut season, becoming the third driver to win the GP2/Formula 2 title in their rookie season after Nico Rosberg and Lewis Hamilton.
Following test driver roles from 2007 to 2009, Hülkenberg signed for Williams in 2010, making his Formula One debut at the Bahrain Grand Prix. After claiming his maiden pole position at the Brazilian Grand Prix, he was replaced by Pastor Maldonado at the end of the season. He joined Force India as a reserve driver in 2011. He was then promoted to a full-time seat for the 2012 season. In 2013, he competed for Sauber, before returning to Force India in 2014 to partner Sergio Pérez. After his 2015 and 2016 campaigns at Force India, Hülkenberg switched to Renault for 2017. Replaced by Esteban Ocon at the end of 2019, he returned to Force India—then known as Racing Point and later re-branded as Aston Martin—as a reserve driver from 2020 to 2022; Hülkenberg substituted for Pérez and Lance Stroll at three Grands Prix in 2020, and for Sebastian Vettel at two in 2022. He returned as a full-time driver with Haas in 2023 and 2024, alongside Kevin Magnussen. Hülkenberg re-joined Sauber for his 2025 campaign, where he achieved his maiden podium at the British Grand Prix, ahead of their acquisition by Audi in 2026.
As of the 2026 Miami Grand Prix, Hülkenberg has achieved one pole position, two fastest laps, and one podium finish in Formula One. He holds the Formula One records for the most starts without a win (253) and the most starts before a podium (239). content.content:
{"title": "2025 World Series", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_World_Series", "lead": "The 2025 World Series (branded as the 2025 World Series presented by Capital One) was the championship series of Major League Baseball's (MLB) 2025 season. The 121st edition of the World Series, it was a best-of-seven playoff between the National League (NL) champion and defending World Series champion Los Angeles Dodgers and the American League (AL) champion Toronto Blue Jays. The series was played from October 24 to November 1. The Dodgers defeated the Blue Jays in seven games to win their second consecutive World Series and ninth overall. They became MLB's first franchise to win back-to-back championships since 2000, the first from the NL since 1976, and the eighth overall. Dodgers pitcher Yoshinobu Yamamoto was named the World Series MVP, recording three wins and a 1.02 ERA. 
Both teams reached the playoffs by winning their respective divisions; the Dodgers won the National League West for the 12th time in 13 seasons, while the Blue Jays won the American League East for the first time since 2015. In the 2025 postseason, the Dodgers went 9–1 in the NL playoffs, while the Blue Jays earned a first-round bye and went 7–4 in the AL. The Blue Jays made their third World Series appearance, and their first in 32 years, after their back-to-back titles in 1992 and 1993. The Dodgers were favored heading into the Series, but the Blue Jays had home-field advantage due to their better regular-season record.
The series was closely contested throughout between both clubs. In Game 1, the Blue Jays scored nine runs in the sixth inning to win uncontested. The Dodgers responded in Game 2 with a complete game by Yamamoto, who gave up just one run. The 18-inning Game 3 was the second-longest game in World Series history by time; Freddie Freeman hit a walk-off home run for the second World Series in a row. However, the Blue Jays seized control with two straight wins in Los Angeles, as the Dodgers bullpen struggled. Back in Toronto, Yamamoto pitching six innings of one-run ball and a critical base-running mistake by Addison Barger tied the series for the final time to force a deciding game. Game 7 was the sixth World Series Game 7 to go into extra innings. With Toronto two outs away from the title, Miguel Rojas tied the game with a solo home run. Will Smith then hit the go-ahead home run in the eleventh. One day after throwing 96 pitches, Yamamoto pitched the final 2+2⁄3 innings to clinch the title for Los Angeles.
The series was televised by Fox in the United States and by Sportsnet in Canada. The series averaged 16.1 million viewers in the U.S., making it the most-watched World Series since 2017. Game 7 was the most-watched World Series game since Game 7 in 1991, with at least 51 million viewers across the U.S., Canada, and Japan.
Several commentators have called the 2025 Series one of the greatest World Series of all time, and its Game 7 one of the greatest World Series games of all time.  Several World Series records were broken, including the first pinch-hit grand slam (Addison Barger, Game 1); the most times reaching base (9) and the most intentional walks (4) for a player in a game (Shohei Ohtani, Game 3); the most innings pitched (73.0) by a team in a best-of-seven matchup (both teams); and the most innings caught in a single Series (Will Smith). In addition, Ernie Clement set the record for the most hits for a player in a single postseason (30). Following the series, several sports outlets hailed the Dodgers as a dynasty.", "error": null}

user_question: What causes a rainbow to appear?

messages.content: What causes a rainbow to appear?

content.text:
A rainbow appears due to a combination of light refraction, reflection, and dispersion in water droplets. Here's how it works:

1. **Sunlight enters water droplets**: When sunlight passes through rain droplets or mist in the air, the light enters the droplet and bends (refracts) because water has a different refractive index than air.

2. **Light reflects inside the droplet**: Once inside the droplet, the light travels to the back surface and reflects off it, bouncing back toward the front of the droplet.

3. **Light exits and bends again**: As the light exits the droplet, it refracts again as it moves from water back into air.

4. **Dispersion creates the colors**: White sunlight is actually composed of all colors of the visible spectrum. Different wavelengths of light (different colors) refract at slightly different angles. Red light bends the least and exits at about 42° from the antisolar point (the point directly opposite the sun from your perspective), while violet light bends the most and exits at about 40°. This separation of colors is called dispersion.

5. **Your viewing angle**: You see a rainbow when you're positioned between the sun and the rain, looking at the water droplets with the sun behind you. The primary rainbow forms at an angle of about 42° from the antisolar point, with red on the outer edge and violet on the inner edge.

**Additional details**: 
- A secondary rainbow can sometimes form from light that reflects twice inside the droplets, appearing at about 50-53° from the antisolar point with the color order reversed.
- Rainbows always appear in a circular arc (though we usually only see part of it), centered on the antisolar point.

final_answer:
A rainbow appears due to a combination of light refraction, reflection, and dispersion in water droplets. Here's how it works:

1. **Sunlight enters water droplets**: When sunlight passes through rain droplets or mist in the air, the light enters the droplet and bends (refracts) because water has a different refractive index than air.

2. **Light reflects inside the droplet**: Once inside the droplet, the light travels to the back surface and reflects off it, bouncing back toward the front of the droplet.

3. **Light exits and bends again**: As the light exits the droplet, it refracts again as it moves from water back into air.

4. **Dispersion creates the colors**: White sunlight is actually composed of all colors of the visible spectrum. Different wavelengths of light (different colors) refract at slightly different angles. Red light bends the least and exits at about 42° from the antisolar point (the point directly opposite the sun from your perspective), while violet light bends the most and exits at about 40°. This separation of colors is called dispersion.

5. **Your viewing angle**: You see a rainbow when you're positioned between the sun and the rain, looking at the water droplets with the sun behind you. The primary rainbow forms at an angle of about 42° from the antisolar point, with red on the outer edge and violet on the inner edge.

**Additional details**: 
- A secondary rainbow can sometimes form from light that reflects twice inside the droplets, appearing at about 50-53° from the antisolar point with the color order reversed.
- Rainbows always appear in a circular arc (though we usually only see part of it), centered on the antisolar point.
golden_answer: Rainbows form when sunlight enters water droplets, refracts, reflects off the back of the droplet, and refracts again on exit, separating into its component colors.

user_question: What's photosynthesis in simple terms?

messages.content: What's photosynthesis in simple terms?

content.text:
# Photosynthesis in Simple Terms

Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy (usually from the sun) into chemical energy stored in food (glucose/sugar).

Here's the basic idea:

**The simple version:**
- Plants take in **sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide** (a gas from the air)
- They use the light energy to transform these ingredients into **glucose (sugar)** and **oxygen**
- The plant uses the sugar for energy and growth
- The oxygen is released back into the air as a byproduct

**Why it matters:**
- It's how plants feed themselves and grow
- It produces the oxygen we breathe
- It's the foundation of most food chains on Earth
- It converts solar energy into a form that living things can use

**Simple equation:**
Sunlight + Water + Carbon Dioxide → Sugar + Oxygen

You can think of it like a solar-powered factory where plants are the factory workers, using sunlight as their power source to create food from raw materials.

A soccer team has **11 players on the field** at a time during a match.

This typically consists of:
- 1 goalkeeper

user_question: Why do humans have fingerprints?

messages.content: Why do humans have fingerprints?

content.text:
Humans have fingerprints for several important reasons:

1. **Grip and Traction**: Fingerprints provide enhanced grip and traction. The ridges on our fingertips increase friction between our skin and objects, making it easier to grasp, hold, and manipulate things. This is particularly useful for tasks requiring fine motor control.

2. **Sensory Function**: The ridges and whorls of fingerprints contain numerous nerve endings that make our fingertips highly sensitive to touch. This enhanced tactile sensitivity allows us to detect subtle textures, temperatures, and pressures, which is crucial for precise manipulation and exploration of our environment.

3. **Water and Moisture Management**: Some research suggests that fingerprints may help with gripping objects in wet conditions. The ridges can channel away water and sweat, maintaining better contact between the skin and objects.

4. **Uniqueness and Identification**: While not an evolutionary purpose per se, fingerprints are unique to each individual, making them valuable for identification. This uniqueness is thought to be a byproduct of the random development patterns of ridge formation during fetal development.

5. **Enhanced Sensitivity to Vibrations**: The ridge patterns may help detect and amplify vibrations, allowing for better perception of subtle sensations.

From an evolutionary perspective, fingerprints likely developed because they provided survival advantages—better grip strength, improved dexterity, and enhanced sensory perception would have been beneficial for our ancestors in manipulating tools, gathering food, and interacting with their environment.

user_question: Who discovered penicillin?

messages.content: Who discovered penicillin?

content.text:
Penicillin was discovered by **Alexander Fleming**, a Scottish bacteriologist. In 1928, Fleming discovered penicillin by accident when he noticed that a mold called *Penicillium notatum* had contaminated one of his bacterial cultures and had killed the surrounding bacteria. This serendipitous observation led to the development of the first widely used antibiotic, which revolutionized medicine and has saved countless lives.

However, it's worth noting that while Fleming discovered penicillin, the work of other scientists—particularly Howard Florey and Ernst Boris Chain—was crucial in isolating, purifying, and developing penicillin for practical medical use during World War II. All three scientists shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1945 for this work.

final_answer:
Penicillin was discovered by **Alexander Fleming**, a Scottish bacteriologist. In 1928, Fleming discovered penicillin by accident when he noticed that a mold called *Penicillium notatum* had contaminated one of his bacterial cultures and had killed the surrounding bacteria. This serendipitous observation led to the development of the first widely used antibiotic, which revolutionized medicine and has saved countless lives.

However, it's worth noting that while Fleming discovered penicillin, the work of other scientists—particularly Howard Florey and Ernst Boris Chain—was crucial in isolating, purifying, and developing penicillin for practical medical use during World War II. All three scientists shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1945 for this work.
golden_answer: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 after noticing that mold contaminating a petri dish killed surrounding bacteria.

user_question: What's the Pythagorean theorem?

messages.content: What's the Pythagorean theorem?

content.text:
The Pythagorean theorem is a fundamental principle in mathematics that describes the relationship between the sides of a right triangle. Here's what you need to know:

**The Theorem:**
In a right triangle, the square of the length of the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle) equals the sum of the squares of the lengths of the other two sides.

**The Formula:**
If we call the two shorter sides *a* and *b*, and the hypotenuse *c*, then:

**a² + b² = c²**

**Example:**
If a right triangle has sides of length 3 and 4, you can find the hypotenuse:
- 3² + 4² = c²
- 9 + 16 = c²
- 25 = c²
- c = 5

So the hypotenuse would be 5 units long.

**Why It Matters:**
The Pythagorean theorem is one of the most important theorems in mathematics. It's used in:
- Construction and engineering
- Navigation and surveying
- Physics and astronomy
- Computer graphics
- And countless other fields

The theorem is named after the ancient Greek mathematician Pythagoras, though the relationship was known to earlier civilizations as well. It's been proven in hundreds of different ways throughout history!

final_answer:

golden_answer: The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides: a² + b² = c².

user_question: What's the freezing point of water?

messages.content: What's the freezing point of water?

content.text:
The freezing point of water is **0°C (32°F)** at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm).

This is the temperature at which water transitions from a liquid state to a solid state (ice). However, it's worth noting that water can remain in a liquid state below 0°C under certain conditions (like when it's supercooled or under pressure), but 0°C is the normal freezing point under standard conditions.

final_answer:
The freezing point of water is **0°C (32°F)** at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm).

However, it's worth noting that water can remain in a liquid state below 0°C under certain conditions (like when it's supercooled or under pressure), but 0°C is the normal freezing point under standard conditions.
golden_answer: Water freezes at 0°C (32°F) at standard atmospheric pressure.

user_question: What's the largest mammal on Earth?

messages.content: What's the largest mammal on Earth?

content.text:
The largest mammal on Earth is the **blue whale**. 

Blue whales can reach lengths of up to 100 feet (30 meters) and can weigh as much as 200 tons (180 metric tonnes), making them not only the largest mammal but the largest animal ever known to have existed. Despite their enormous size, blue whales are baleen whales that feed on tiny krill (small shrimp-like creatures), filtering them from the ocean water through their baleen plates.

final_answer:
The largest mammal on Earth is the **blue whale**. 

Blue whales can reach lengths of up to 100 feet (30 meters) and can weigh as much as 200 tons (180 metric tonnes), making them not only the largest mammal but the largest animal ever known to have existed. Despite their enormous size, blue whales are baleen whales that feed on tiny krill (small shrimp-like creatures), filtering them from the ocean water through their baleen plates.
golden_answer: The blue whale is the largest mammal — and the largest animal known to have ever existed — reaching lengths of up to 30 meters.

messages.content: When did Infinity Castle actually hit theaters and who handled the international release?

content.text: I'll search for information about Infinity Castle to find out when it was released and who handled the international distribution.

content.content:
{"title": "Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba – The Movie: Infinity Castle", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demon_Slayer:_Kimetsu_no_Yaiba_%E2%80%93_The_Movie:_Infinity_Castle", "lead": "Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba – The Movie: Infinity Castle (Japanese: 劇場版「鬼滅の刃」無限城編, Hepburn: Gekijō-ban Kimetsu no Yaiba: Mugen Jō-hen) is a 2025 Japanese animated dark fantasy action film based on the Infinity Castle arc of the 2016–20 manga series Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba by Koyoharu Gotouge. It is a direct sequel to the fourth season of the anime television series as well as the manga fourth, fifth, and sixth adaptations, following the film Mugen Train (2020) and the feature-length compilations To the Swordsmith Village (2023) and To the Hashira Training (2024). It is directed by Haruo Sotozaki, produced by Ufotable, and written by the studio's staff members. It follows the Demon Slayer Corps in their decisive climactic battle against Muzan Kibutsuji and the remaining Upper Ranks within the titular "Infinity Castle".
Unlike the Swordsmith Village and Hashira Training adaptations, which are compilations of episodes from the anime series, Infinity Castle is a feature-length multi-film adaptation due to the arc's content and dramatic pacing, similarly to Mugen Train. It is the first film of a trilogy announced in June 2024, immediately following the airing of the fourth season's finale.
It was released in Japan on July 18, 2025, by Aniplex and Toho. It was released by Crunchyroll through Sony Pictures Releasing in select Asian countries in August, before releasing in all other territories the following month. The film became the seventh-highest-grossing film of 2025, grossing $781 million worldwide, and the highest-grossing international film in the U.S. (previously held by Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (2000)). The film also broke multiple box-office records, including becoming the highest-grossing Japanese film, the highest-grossing R-rated animated film, the second-highest-grossing adult animated film and the first R-rated non-American and animated film to gross over $700 million mark, as well as overtaking The Simpsons Movie (2007) as the highest-grossing film based on an animated television series and the second highest-grossing traditionally animated film, behind The Lion King (1994). It's also the highest-grossing traditionally animated film in its initial release, surpassing the initial gross of The Lion King. The film received generally positive reviews from critics, who praised its action sequences, visuals, and engaging story, although the pacing received some criticism. The film won Best Animation Film at the 49th Japan Academy Film Prize, and was nominated for Best Motion Picture – Animated at the 83rd Golden Globe Awards.

golden_answer: Vincent van Gogh painted 'The Starry Night' in 1889 while staying at an asylum in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence, France.

user_question: What gas do plants absorb from the air?

messages.content: What gas do plants absorb from the air?

final_answer: Plants absorb **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** from the air. They use this gas in the process of photosynthesis, where they convert CO₂ and water into glucose (sugar) and oxygen, using energy from sunlight. The oxygen is then released back into the air as a byproduct.
golden_answer: Plants absorb carbon dioxide (CO₂) from the air and use it during photosynthesis.

user_question: Why does ice float on water?

messages.content: Why does ice float on water?

content.text:
Ice floats on water because of a unique property of water: **ice is less dense than liquid water**.

Here's why this happens:

1. **Hydrogen bonding structure**: When water freezes, the water molecules arrange themselves into a crystalline structure held together by hydrogen bonds. In this solid structure, the molecules are spaced farther apart than they are in liquid water.

2. **Lower density of ice**: Because the molecules are more spread out in ice compared to liquid water, ice has a lower density. Since less dense objects float on more dense liquids, ice floats on water.

3. **Anomalous expansion**: Water is unusual because most substances become denser when they freeze. Water, however, becomes *less* dense when it freezes – this is called anomalous expansion or the anomaly of water.

4. **The hexagonal crystal structure**: The crystalline arrangement of ice molecules forms a hexagonal lattice with more empty space compared to the tightly packed arrangement of molecules in liquid water.

This is actually very important for life on Earth! If ice sank instead of floated, lakes and oceans would freeze from the bottom up, which could be devastating for aquatic ecosystems. Instead, ice floats on top and acts as an insulating layer, protecting the liquid water (and the organisms in it) below from extreme cold.

final_answer:
Ice floats on water because of a unique property of water: **ice is less dense than liquid water**.

Here's why this happens:

1. **Hydrogen bonding structure**: When water freezes, the water molecules arrange themselves into a crystalline structure held together by hydrogen bonds. In this solid structure, the molecules are spaced farther apart than they are in liquid water.

2. **Lower density of ice**: Because the molecules are more spread out in ice compared to liquid water, ice has a lower density. Since less dense objects float on more dense liquids, ice floats on water.

3. **Anomalous expansion**: Water is unusual because most substances become denser when they freeze. Water, however, becomes *less* dense when it freezes – this is called anomalous expansion or the anomaly of water.

4. **The hexagonal crystal structure**: The crystalline arrangement of ice molecules forms a hexagonal lattice with more empty space compared to the tightly packed arrangement of molecules in liquid water.

This is actually very important for life on Earth! If ice sank instead of floated, lakes and oceans would freeze from the bottom up, which could be devastating for aquatic ecosystems. Instead, ice floats on top and acts as an insulating layer, protecting the liquid water (and the organisms in it) below from extreme cold.
golden_answer: Water molecules form a crystalline structure when frozen that takes up more space than liquid water, making ice less dense and causing it to float.

messages.content: How many planets are in our solar system?

content.text:
There are **8 planets** in our solar system. In order from the Sun, they are:

1. Mercury
2. Venus
3. Earth
4. Mars
5. Jupiter
6. Saturn
7. Uranus
8. Neptune

It's worth noting that Pluto was reclassified from a planet to a "dwarf planet" in 2006 by the International Astronomical Union, which is why it's no longer counted among the main planets.

final_answer:
There are **8 planets** in our solar system. In order from the Sun, they are:

1. Mercury
2. Venus
3. Earth
4. Mars
5. Jupiter
6. Saturn
7. Uranus
8. Neptune

It's worth noting that Pluto was reclassified from a planet to a "dwarf planet" in 2006 by the International Astronomical Union, which is why it's no longer counted among the main planets.
golden_answer: There are eight planets in our solar system since Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.

The all-sources betting handle on the Derby and the entire racing card set records of $234.4 million and $349 million, respectively. It is the 14th television series in the Marvel Cinematic Universe (MCU) from Marvel Studios and was produced under its Marvel Television label. Sharing continuity with the films of the MCU, the series begins after the events of the film Black Panther: Wakanda Forever (2022) and follows MIT student Riri Williams as she returns home to Chicago where she discovers secrets that pit technology against magic. Hodge served as head writer for the series, which was also produced by Proximity Media.\nDominique Thorne reprises her role as Riri Williams / Ironheart from Wakanda Forever, starring alongside Lyric Ross, Manny Montana, Matthew Elam, Anji White, Jim Rash, Eric Andr\u00e9, Cree Summer, Sonia Denis, Shea Coule\u00e9, Zoe Terakes, Shakira Barrera, Anthony Ramos, Alden Ehrenreich, Regan Aliyah, Paul Calder\u00f3n, and Sacha Baron Cohen. The series was announced in December 2020, along with Thorne's casting. Hodge was hired in April 2021, with additional castings revealed in February 2022. Sam Bailey and Angela Barnes joined to direct in April 2022. Filming began at Trilith Studios in Atlanta, Georgia, by early June, before moving to Chicago in late October, and concluded by early November.\nIronheart premiered on Disney+ with its first three episodes on June 24, 2025, followed by its other three episodes on July 1. It is the conclusion of Phase Five of the MCU. The series received generally positive reviews from critics, but became subject to a review bombing campaign ahead of its release.", "error": null}

user_question: What's the deepest part of the ocean?

messages.content: What's the deepest part of the ocean?

content.text:
The deepest part of the ocean is the **Mariana Trench**, specifically a location called the **Challenger Deep**. It reaches a depth of approximately **10,994 meters (36,070 feet)** below sea level.

The Mariana Trench is located in the western Pacific Ocean, near the island of Guam, and forms where the Pacific Plate meets the Mariana Plate at a subduction zone. The Challenger Deep, named after the HMS Challenger research ship, is the deepest known point in any ocean on Earth.

final_answer:
The deepest part of the ocean is the **Mariana Trench**, specifically a location called the **Challenger Deep**. It reaches a depth of approximately **10,994 meters (36,070 feet)** below sea level.

The Mariana Trench is located in the western Pacific Ocean, near the island of Guam, and forms where the Pacific Plate meets the Mariana Plate at a subduction zone. The Challenger Deep, named after the HMS Challenger research ship, is the deepest known point in any ocean on Earth.
golden_answer: The Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench is the deepest known point, reaching about 11,000 meters below sea level.

user_question: Who wrote 'War and Peace'?

messages.content: Who wrote 'War and Peace'?

content.text: Leo Tolstoy wrote "War and Peace." It's one of the most famous novels in world literature, published in the 1860s. The epic novel is set during the Napoleonic Wars and follows the lives of several aristocratic Russian families, exploring themes of history, love, war, and the human condition.

final_answer: Leo Tolstoy wrote "War and Peace." It's one of the most famous novels in world literature, published in the 1860s. The epic novel is set during the Napoleonic Wars and follows the lives of several aristocratic Russian families, exploring themes of history, love, war, and the human condition.
golden_answer: Leo Tolstoy wrote 'War and Peace', published in full in 1869.

{"title": "Lando Norris", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lando_Norris", "lead": "Lando Norris ( ; born 13 November 1999) is a  British racing driver who competes in Formula One for McLaren. Norris won the Formula One World Drivers' Championship in 2025 with McLaren, and has won 11 Grands Prix across eight seasons.
Born in Bristol and raised in Glastonbury to an English father and Belgian mother, Norris began competitive kart racing aged eight. After a successful karting career—culminating in his victory at the senior World Championship in 2014—Norris graduated to junior formulae. He won his first title at the 2015 MSA Formula Championship with Carlin. He then won the Toyota Racing Series, Formula Renault Eurocup, and Formula Renault NEC in 2016, receiving the Autosport BRDC Award that year. Norris won the FIA Formula 3 European Championship in 2017, and finished runner-up to George Russell in the FIA Formula 2 Championship in 2018, both with Carlin.
A member of the McLaren Young Driver Programme since 2017, Norris joined McLaren in 2019 to partner Carlos Sainz Jr., making his Formula One debut at the Australian Grand Prix. He achieved his maiden podium finish and fastest lap at the season-opening Austrian Grand Prix in 2020, before achieving his maiden pole position at the Russian Grand Prix in 2021, amongst several further podiums. Following another podium in 2022, he took seven across his 2023 campaign. In 2024, Norris achieved his maiden win at the Miami Grand Prix, repeating this feat three times as he finished runner-up to Max Verstappen in the World Drivers' Championship. He took seven further victories in 2025, including his home Grand Prix in Britain, as he won his maiden title in a battle with Verstappen and teammate Oscar Piastri.
As of the 2026 Miami Grand Prix, Norris has achieved 11 race wins, 16 pole positions, 19 fastest laps, and 45 podiums in Formula One. He is contracted to remain at McLaren until at least the end of the 2027 season. Norris was listed in the 2026 edition of Time as one of the 100 most influential people in the world.", "error": null}

It was the twenty-first and penultimate round of the 2022 Formula One World Championship and the third and final Grand Prix weekend in the season to utilise the sprint format.\nMercedes driver George Russell took his maiden Grand Prix win with his teammate Lewis Hamilton in second and with Ferrari driver Carlos Sainz Jr. completing the podium in third. This race marked the first time since the 2010 Canadian Grand Prix that British drivers finished first and second in a Grand Prix. The race was also the last before Red Bull Racing started a record-breaking run of 15 consecutive race wins which lasted for 10 months until Carlos Sainz Jr. won the Singapore Grand Prix the next season. This was Russell's and Mercedes's last win until the 2024 Austrian Grand Prix.", "error": null}

user_question: Who is credited with inventing the light bulb?

messages.content: Who is credited with inventing the light bulb?

content.text:
I can answer this from my knowledge. **Thomas Edison** is most famously credited with inventing the practical, commercially viable light bulb. In 1879, he developed an incandescent light bulb with a carbonized cotton filament that could burn for extended periods, making it practical for widespread use.

However, it's worth noting that Edison wasn't the first to develop a light bulb concept. Several inventors contributed to its development, including:
- **Humphry Davy** - created the first electric light (an arc lamp) in 1802
- **Warren de la Rue** - developed an early light bulb using a coiled carbon filament in 1874
- **Joseph Swan** - created a similar incandescent bulb around the same time as Edison

But Edison is credited with the invention because he created the first *practical* light bulb that could be manufactured affordably and used reliably in homes and businesses. He also developed the electrical infrastructure to support widespread use of electric lights.

final_answer:
I can answer this from my knowledge. **Thomas Edison** is most famously credited with inventing the practical, commercially viable light bulb. In 1879, he developed an incandescent light bulb with a carbonized cotton filament that could burn for extended periods, making it practical for widespread use.

However, it's worth noting that Edison wasn't the first to develop a light bulb concept. Several inventors contributed to its development, including:
- **Humphry Davy** - created the first electric light (an arc lamp) in 1802
- **Warren de la Rue** - developed an early light bulb using a coiled carbon filament in 1874
- **Joseph Swan** - created a similar incandescent bulb around the same time as Edison

But Edison is credited with the invention because he created the first *practical* light bulb that could be manufactured affordably and used reliably in homes and businesses. He also developed the electrical infrastructure to support widespread use of electric lights.
golden_answer: Thomas Edison is most famously credited with inventing a practical incandescent light bulb in 1879, though others like Joseph Swan developed similar technology around the same time.

The World Championships are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating. The first World Championships were held in 1896 in Saint Petersburg, Russia, and they have been held ever since with only four interruptions. A separate competition for women was established in 1905, with the men's and women's events held as separate competitions for several years. Pair skating was added in 1908 and ice dance in 1952. Skaters are eligible to compete at the World Championships, provided they represent a member nation of the International Skating Union and are selected by their respective federation.   \nThe corresponding competition for junior-level skaters is the World Junior Figure Skating Championships. The corresponding competitions for synchronized skating are the World Synchronized Skating Championships and the World Junior Synchronized Skating Championships.\nMedals are awarded in men's singles, women's singles, pair skating, and ice dance. Ulrich Salchow of Sweden holds the record for winning the most World Championship titles in men's singles (with ten), while Sonja Henie of Norway holds the record in women's singles (also with ten). Irina Rodnina and Alexander Zaitsev of the Soviet Union hold the record in pair skating (with six), although Rodnina won an additional four titles with a previous partner. Lyudmila Pakhomova and Aleksandr Gorshkov of the Soviet Union hold the record in ice dance (with six), while Guillaume Cizeron of France has also won six titles in ice dance, but not with the same partner.", "error": null}

{"title": "Ironheart (miniseries)", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ironheart_(miniseries)", "lead": "Ironheart is an American television miniseries created by Chinaka Hodge for the streaming service Disney+, based on Marvel Comics featuring the character of the same name. Sharing continuity with the films of the MCU, the series begins after the events of the film Black Panther: Wakanda Forever (2022) and follows MIT student Riri Williams as she returns home to Chicago where she discovers secrets that pit technology against magic. Hodge served as head writer for the series, which was also produced by Proximity Media.\nDominique Thorne reprises her role as Riri Williams / Ironheart from Wakanda Forever, starring alongside Lyric Ross, Manny Montana, Matthew Elam, Anji White, Jim Rash, Eric Andr\u00e9, Cree Summer, Sonia Denis, Shea Coule\u00e9, Zoe Terakes, Shakira Barrera, Anthony Ramos, Alden Ehrenreich, Regan Aliyah, Paul Calder\u00f3n, and Sacha Baron Cohen. The series was announced in December 2020, along with Thorne's casting. Hodge was hired in April 2021, with additional castings revealed in February 2022. Sam Bailey and Angela Barnes joined to direct in April 2022. Filming began at Trilith Studios in Atlanta, Georgia, by early June, before moving to Chicago in late October, and concluded by early November.\nIronheart premiered on Disney+ with its first three episodes on June 24, 2025, followed by its other three episodes on July 1. It is the conclusion of Phase Five of the MCU. The series received generally positive reviews from critics, but became subject to a review bombing campaign ahead of its release.", "error": null}

{"title": "2025 World Snooker Championship", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_World_Snooker_Championship", "lead": "The 2025 World Snooker Championship (officially the 2025 Halo World Snooker Championship) was a professional snooker tournament that took place from 19 April to 5 May 2025 at the Crucible Theatre in Sheffield, England, the 49th consecutive year that the World Snooker Championship was staged at the venue. Organised by the World Snooker Tour and sponsored for the first time by technology company Halo Service Solutions, the tournament was the 18th and final ranking event of the 2024‍–‍25 season. It was broadcast domestically by BBC Sport, in Europe by Eurosport, and elsewhere in the world by WST Play and other broadcasters. The winner received £500,000 from a total prize fund of £2,395,000.
The top 16 players from the snooker world rankings—as they stood after the 2025 Tour Championship—were seeded through to the main stage at the Crucible. They were joined by the 16 successful players from the qualifying rounds, which took place from 7 to 16 April at the English Institute of Sport in Sheffield, featuring 128 professional and invited amateur competitors. A then-record number of players from China—four seeds and six qualifiers, making ten in total—reached the main stage of the tournament. Crucible debutants at the event were Lei Peifan, Zak Surety, and Daniel Wells. Veteran players Dominic Dale and Joe Perry, who had both played on the professional tour since 1992, retired after their qualifying defeats.
Kyren Wilson was the defending champion, having defeated Jak Jones 18‍–‍14 in the 2024 final to win his maiden world title. He lost 9‍–‍10 to Lei in the first round, becoming the 20th player to experience the so-called Crucible curse, referring to the fact that no first-time champion had retained the title since the tournament moved to the Crucible in 1977. Competing as an amateur after serving a 20-month ban, Zhao Xintong won four qualifying matches to reach the main stage. After beating Ronnie O'Sullivan 17‍–‍7 in the semi-finals, Zhao defeated Mark Williams 18–12 in the final to win his first world title, second Triple Crown title, and third ranking title. The first World Champion from China, as well as the first from Asia, he was the fourth qualifier to win the world title and the first player to win a ranking title while competing as an amateur. Williams, aged 50, was the oldest finalist in the tournament's history, surpassing Ray Reardon, who had reached the 1982 final at age 49. The final featured the largest ever age gap (22 years) between two world finalists and was also the first world final contested by two left-handed players. 
The main stage of the tournament produced 107 century breaks, the third-highest total on record, and the qualifying rounds produced a new record of 143 centuries. Zhao made 18 centuries across the qualifying rounds and main stage combined, equalling the record set by Ding Junhui at the 2016 event. While playing Allan Taylor in the third qualifying round, Jackson Page became the first player to make two maximum breaks in a professional match. He won a £147,000 bonus on offer for making two maximums across that season's Triple Crown events and the Saudi Arabia Snooker Masters; he also won a £10,000 bonus for achieving a maximum in the World Championship qualifiers. Mark Allen made the fifth maximum break of his career and the 15th in Crucible history during his second-round match against Chris Wakelin, winning a £40,000 bonus. These maximums took the season total to a new record of 15, surpassing the previous record of 13. Judd Trump made his 100th century of the season in his second-round match against Shaun Murphy, winning a £100,000 bonus; he finished the season with a record 107 centuries, surpassing Neil Robertson's previous record of 103.", "error": null}

user_question: What's the most spoken language in the world by total speakers?

messages.content: What's the most spoken language in the world by total speakers?

content.text:
Based on my knowledge, **Mandarin Chinese** is the most spoken language in the world by total number of speakers. It has approximately 1.1+ billion speakers globally, primarily concentrated in mainland China, Taiwan, and Chinese diaspora communities worldwide.

This is followed by:
- **Spanish** (~500+ million speakers)
- **English** (~500+ million speakers)
- **Hindi** (~300+ million speakers)

The ranking can vary slightly depending on whether you count native speakers only or include second-language speakers. If counting only native speakers, Mandarin Chinese remains first, though English and Spanish have very large numbers of non-native speakers as well.

final_answer:
Based on my knowledge, **Mandarin Chinese** is the most spoken language in the world by total number of speakers. It has approximately 1.1+ billion speakers globally, primarily concentrated in mainland China, Taiwan, and Chinese diaspora communities worldwide.

This is followed by:
- **Spanish** (~500+ million speakers)
- **English** (~500+ million speakers)
- **Hindi** (~300+ million speakers)

The ranking can vary slightly depending on whether you count native speakers only or include second-language speakers. If counting only native speakers, Mandarin Chinese remains first, though English and Spanish have very large numbers of non-native speakers as well.
golden_answer: English has the most total speakers worldwide when counting both native and second-language speakers, at around 1.5 billion.

user_question: How long is a marathon?

messages.content: How long is a marathon?

content.text:
A marathon is **26.2 miles** (or 42.195 kilometers).

This standard distance has been used for modern marathons since the 1908 Summer Olympics in London. The distance was originally inspired by the legend of a Greek messenger who ran from the town of Marathon to Athens to announce victory in battle, though the actual distance between those two locations is different from the modern marathon distance.

final_answer:
A marathon is **26.2 miles** (or 42.195 kilometers).

This standard distance has been used for modern marathons since the 1908 Summer Olympics in London. The distance was originally inspired by the legend of a Greek messenger who ran from the town of Marathon to Athens to announce victory in battle, though the actual distance between those two locations is different from the modern marathon distance.
golden_answer: A marathon is 42.195 kilometers, or 26.2 miles.
